parts.content: Describe the relationship between substrate concentration and rate of reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Rate of reaction increases as substrate concentration increases (e.g. from 1.1 µmol/min at 0.5 mM to 8.5 µmol/min at 4.0 mM). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At higher substrate concentrations (about 8–10 mM), the rate levels off/approaches a maximum of about 9.0–9.1 µmol/min (plateau). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable additional detail: at low substrate concentration, substrate is limiting so increasing substrate increases enzyme–substrate complex formation/collision frequency. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable additional detail: the plateau indicates the reaction is nearing a maximum rate (Vmax) for the fixed enzyme concentration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the rate of reaction levels off at high substrate concentrations.
parts.markscheme:
- At high substrate concentrations, most/all enzyme active sites are occupied (enzyme saturation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The enzyme is working at/near its maximum rate (Vmax), so adding more substrate does not increase the rate further. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable additional detail: enzyme concentration becomes the limiting factor once saturated (more enzyme would be needed to increase rate). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acceptable additional detail: rate is then limited by the enzyme’s turnover number/catalytic cycle (time needed to convert substrate to product and release product). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Outline the induced-fit model of enzyme-substrate interaction.
parts.markscheme:
- The active site is not a perfect/rigid fit for the substrate initially <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding of the substrate induces a change in enzyme/active site shape (conformational change) to achieve a closer fit <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The conformational change helps position catalytic groups/strain bonds to facilitate catalysis and lower activation energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how end-product inhibition can be used to regulate the rate of a metabolic pathway.
parts.markscheme:
- The final product of a pathway binds to an allosteric (regulatory) site of an enzyme early in the pathway (often the first committed enzyme) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allosteric binding changes the enzyme conformation/active site so the substrate binds less effectively or not at all <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This reduces enzyme activity and slows/stops the pathway when product concentration is high <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Negative feedback prevents over-accumulation of product and conserves cellular resources/energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the difference in product concentration between the control and curve B at 10 minutes.
parts.markscheme: $40\text{ }\mu\text{mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the time point
steps.content: To find the difference in product concentration, we first need to look at the x-axis (Time) and locate the point for **10 minutes**.

steps.title: Read control concentration
steps.content:
Follow the vertical line up from 10 minutes to the **control** curve. Then, look across to the y-axis to find the concentration:

$$\text{Control concentration at 10 min} = 100\text{ }\mu\text{mol}$$

steps.title: Read curve B concentration
steps.content:
Now, follow the same vertical line at 10 minutes to **curve B**. Look across to the y-axis to find its concentration:

$$\text{Curve B concentration at 10 min} = 60\text{ }\mu\text{mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate the difference
steps.content:
Subtract the concentration of curve B from the control concentration to find the difference:

$$\text{Difference} = 100 - 60 = 40\text{ }\mu\text{mol}$$

This calculation aligns with Topic **C1.1 Enzymes and metabolism**, where reading and interpreting enzyme activity graphs is a key skill.

parts.content: Describe the effect of a competitive inhibitor on the rate of reaction during the first 5 minutes.
parts.markscheme:
- The initial rate of reaction is slower compared to the control <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The inhibitor binds to the active site, preventing the substrate from binding <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph gradient
steps.content:
To determine the effect on the rate of reaction, we look at the gradient (slope) of the curves during the **first 5 minutes**. In a graph of product concentration against time, the gradient represents the rate of reaction:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{\Delta [\text{Product}]}{\Delta t}$$

Comparing the "Competitive Inhibitor" curve to the "Control" curve, we can see that the slope is shallower for the inhibitor during this initial period.

steps.title: Identify slower reaction rate
steps.content:
Because the gradient of the competitive inhibitor curve is less steep than the control, we can conclude that the presence of the inhibitor causes a decrease in the speed of the reaction. 

In IB Biology, it is crucial to specify that this is the **initial rate of reaction** that is being affected.

highlights.text: The initial rate of reaction is slower compared to the control

steps.title: Explain the molecular mechanism
steps.content:
This slower rate occurs because a **competitive inhibitor** has a chemical structure similar to the substrate. As a result, it competes with the substrate to **bind to the active site** of the enzyme.

When the inhibitor occupies the active site, it prevents the substrate from binding and reacting, which reduces the overall number of successful enzyme-substrate collisions per unit of time.

highlights.text: The inhibitor binds to the active site, preventing the substrate from binding

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks for this question, you must mention both the observation from the graph and the underlying biological cause:

1.  **Observation:** The initial rate of reaction is slower compared to the control.
2.  **Mechanism:** The inhibitor binds to the active site, preventing the substrate from binding.

For more background on this mechanism, you can review **Topic C1.1.15: Competitive inhibition** in your syllabus.

parts.content: Explain how the shape of the enzyme's active site relates to the effect of a non-competitive inhibitor.
parts.markscheme:
- The inhibitor binds to an allosteric site rather than the active site <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This distorts the shape of the active site so that the substrate can no longer bind <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the binding site
steps.content: In non-competitive inhibition, the inhibitor does not compete with the substrate for the active site. Instead, it binds to a specific location on the enzyme known as the **allosteric site**.

steps.title: Describe the conformational change
steps.content: When the inhibitor binds to this allosteric site, it causes a **conformational change** in the protein. This means the overall three-dimensional shape of the enzyme molecule is altered or distorted.

steps.title: Link to active site
steps.content: Because the enzyme's structure is shifted, the **shape of the active site** changes as well. It is no longer complementary to the substrate, meaning the substrate can no longer bind to the enzyme to undergo a reaction.

steps.title: Final answer breakdown
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks for this question, your explanation should clearly state:
1. The inhibitor binds to the **allosteric site** (not the active site).
2. This binding **distorts/changes the shape of the active site**, preventing the substrate from binding.

highlights.text: The inhibitor binds to an allosteric site rather than the active site
This distorts the shape of the active site so that the substrate can no longer bind

parts.content: Suggest one experimental method to distinguish between competitive and non-competitive inhibition using this data.
parts.markscheme:
- Increase substrate concentration ($[S]$): if the inhibition is overcome/the rate increases to $V_{\text{max}}$, it is competitive; if the rate remains low, it is non-competitive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analysis of $V_{\text{max}}$ and $K_m$: competitive inhibitors increase $K_m$ but $V_{\text{max}}$ remains the same, while non-competitive inhibitors decrease $V_{\text{max}}$ and $K_m$ remains constant <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the experimental variable
steps.content:
To distinguish between the two types of inhibition using the provided graph, we must look at how the enzyme behaves when we change the **substrate concentration** ($[S]$).

This falls under **Topic C1.1: Enzymes and Metabolism**. Specifically, we are looking at how molecular collisions and binding sites are affected by different concentrations of molecules.

steps.title: Vary substrate concentration
steps.content:
The experimental method involves setting up multiple test tubes with a fixed concentration of enzyme and inhibitor, then progressively **increasing the substrate concentration** ($[S]$).

By measuring the rate of reaction at these different levels, we can see if the inhibition can be "overcome" by sheer numbers of substrate molecules.

steps.title: Analyze competitive inhibition results
steps.content:
If the inhibitor is **competitive**, it binds to the active site. If we add enough substrate, the substrate molecules will eventually outcompete the inhibitor for those sites.

In your graph, this is shown by the curve that eventually reaches the **original $V_{\text{max}}$**. Even though the reaction starts slower (indicated by an increased $K_m$), the maximum possible rate remains the same.

highlights.text: if the inhibition is overcome/the rate increases to $V_{\text{max}}$, it is competitive

steps.title: Analyze non-competitive inhibition results
steps.content:
If the inhibitor is **non-competitive**, it binds to an allosteric site, changing the shape of the active site. No matter how much substrate you add, those "poisoned" enzymes cannot function effectively.

In the graph, this is represented by the curve where the **rate remains low** and the $V_{\text{max}}$ is significantly lower than the uninhibited enzyme, regardless of the substrate concentration.

highlights.text: if the rate remains low, it is non-competitive

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this IB question, you should clearly state:
1.  **Method**: Increase the substrate concentration ($[S]$).
2.  **Observation**: If the $V_{\text{max}}$ is eventually reached, it's competitive; if $V_{\text{max}}$ is lower than the control, it's non-competitive.

steps.title: Define kinetic parameters
steps.content:
To distinguish between these inhibitors using the graph, we need to focus on two key kinetic parameters covered in **Topic C1.1 Enzymes and Metabolism**:

1.  **$V_{\text{max}}$**: The maximum rate of reaction when the enzyme is saturated with substrate.
2.  **$K_m$ (Michaelis constant)**: The substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of $V_{\text{max}}$. It represents the affinity of the enzyme for its substrate.

steps.title: Vary substrate concentration
steps.content:
The standard experimental method is to **increase the substrate concentration ($[S]$)** to very high levels while keeping the inhibitor concentration constant. 

By observing whether the reaction rate can eventually reach the original $V_{\text{max}}$, we can identify the type of inhibition.

steps.title: Identify competitive inhibition
steps.content:
If the inhibitor is **competitive**, it binds to the active site. If we add enough substrate, the substrate molecules will out-compete the inhibitor for those sites. 

In this case:
*   **$V_{\text{max}}$ remains the same** as the control (the curve eventually reaches the same height).
*   **$K_m$ increases** (it takes a higher $[S]$ to reach half-maximum speed).

highlights.text: competitive inhibitors increase $K_m$ but $V_{\text{max}}$ remains the same

steps.title: Identify non-competitive inhibition
steps.content:
If the inhibitor is **non-competitive**, it binds to an allosteric site, changing the enzyme's shape. No matter how much substrate you add, those inhibited enzymes stay inactive.

In this case:
*   **$V_{\text{max}}$ decreases** (the curve plateaus at a much lower level).
*   **$K_m$ remains constant** (the affinity of the remaining functional enzymes for the substrate does not change).

highlights.text: non-competitive inhibitors decrease $V_{\text{max}}$ and $K_m$ remains constant

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam:
*   **1 mark** for suggesting the method: Increase substrate concentration to see if the inhibition is overcome.
*   **1 mark** for the correct kinetic comparison: Stating that competitive inhibition results in the same $V_{\text{max}}$ but a higher $K_m$, whereas non-competitive results in a lower $V_{\text{max}}$ but the same $K_m$.

parts.markscheme:
- Rate of reaction increases as temperature increases from 10°C (1.2 µmol/min) to 40°C (7.5 µmol/min).
- Optimum temperature is about 40°C (maximum rate recorded).
- Above 40°C the rate decreases (e.g., drops from 7.5 at 40°C to 3.0 at 50°C).

<marks>2 marks</marks>: <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the increase to a peak/optimum at about 40°C; <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing the decrease in rate above 40°C (with appropriate reference to data).

parts.content: Explain why the enzyme activity decreases at temperatures above 40°C.
parts.markscheme:
- Higher temperature increases molecular kinetic energy/vibration, which disrupts weak bonds (e.g. hydrogen and ionic bonds) stabilising the enzyme’s tertiary structure.
- Enzyme denatures (3D structure changes) at temperatures above the optimum.
- Active site changes shape so it is no longer complementary to the substrate.
- Fewer enzyme–substrate complexes form, so the reaction rate decreases (denaturation may be irreversible).

<marks>2 marks</marks>: <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining denaturation due to disruption of bonds/tertiary structure at high temperature; <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking denaturation to altered active site/reduced enzyme–substrate complex formation and hence reduced rate.

questions.specification: Metabolic pathways are central to cellular metabolism.

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Describe the roles of enzymes in regulating metabolic pathways.
parts.markscheme:
- Enzymes lower activation energy to increase reaction rate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Substrate specificity ensures correct sequence of reactions (specific active site) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enzyme activity can be increased/decreased by inhibitors or activators (including allosteric regulation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- End-product (feedback) inhibition regulates pathway by inhibiting an earlier enzyme <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enzyme concentration affects pathway flux/rate (more enzyme increases rate up to limiting factors) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how ATP production is linked to metabolic pathways in cells.
parts.markscheme:
- ATP is produced during respiration, which is a series of enzyme-catalysed metabolic pathways (glycolysis, link reaction, Krebs cycle, electron transport chain) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glycolysis produces a small yield of ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Krebs cycle produces reduced coenzymes (NADH and FADH$_2$) that carry electrons to the electron transport chain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electron transport transfers electrons through carriers in the membrane, releasing energy used to pump H$^+$ across the membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The resulting proton gradient (electrochemical gradient) is used by ATP synthase (chemiosmosis) to phosphorylate ADP to ATP <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxygen is the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration, allowing continued electron flow and ATP production; water is formed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In anaerobic conditions, limited ATP is produced because the electron transport chain cannot operate; fermentation regenerates NAD$^+$ to allow glycolysis to continue <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Enzymes provide an alternative reaction pathway / route. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The alternative pathway has a lower activation energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enzymes stabilize the transition state / lower the energy of the transition state. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The energy difference between reactants and products remains the same (the enzyme does not change the energy released/absorbed in the reaction). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accept annotations on the graph showing the reduced activation energy peak. <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.1 Enzymes and metabolism > C1.1.15 Competitive inhibition (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.1.15 Competitive inhibition (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires analyzing experimental data to distinguish between types of enzyme inhibition. Since the rate of reaction with the inhibitor approaches the Vmax of the reaction without the inhibitor at high substrate concentrations (118 vs 120), the data describes competitive inhibition.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.1 Enzymes and metabolism > C1.1.8 Effects of temperature, pH and substrate concentration on the rate of enzyme activity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.1.8 Effects of temperature, pH and substrate concentration on the rate of enzyme activity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question involves interpreting a table that shows the effect of substrate concentration on the rate of an enzyme-catalyzed reaction, which is the specific subject of this topic.

options.content: Substance X is a competitive inhibitor.
options.markscheme: Correct. In competitive inhibition, inhibitor and substrate compete for the active site. Increasing substrate concentration can overcome inhibition, reaching the same $V_{max}$.

options.content: Substance X is a non-competitive inhibitor.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Non-competitive inhibitors reduce $V_{max}$ because inhibition cannot be overcome by increasing substrate concentration.

options.content: Substance X binds to an allosteric site.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Allosteric binding is typically associated with non-competitive inhibition; the data show $V_{max}$ is approached at high substrate concentration.

options.content: Substance X irreversibly denatures the enzyme.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Irreversible denaturation would prevent recovery of reaction rate at high substrate concentrations.

parts.content: Describe how pH affects enzyme activity.
parts.markscheme:
- each enzyme has an optimal pH <marks>1 mark</marks>
- deviation from the optimal pH decreases activity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- pH affects the ionisation of amino acid side chains <marks>1 mark</marks>
- altered charges affect binding of the substrate and the enzyme's structure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- extreme pH leads to denaturation of the enzyme <marks>1 mark</marks>
- denaturation distorts the shape of the active site <marks>1 mark</marks>
- results in a loss of function <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State four factors affecting the rate of enzyme-catalysed reactions.
parts.markscheme:
- temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>
- pH <marks>1 mark</marks>
- substrate concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- enzyme concentration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- presence of inhibitors <marks>1 mark</marks>
- availability of cofactors or coenzymes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- salinity of the solution <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the effect of temperature on the rate of enzyme activity.
parts.markscheme:
- higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>
- results in more frequent collisions between the enzyme and substrate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- more enzyme-substrate complexes are formed per unit time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- rate of reaction increases until the enzyme reaches its optimal temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the optimal temperature produces the highest reaction rate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- above the optimal temperature, thermal energy causes bonds in the enzyme to break <marks>1 mark</marks>
- leads to changes in the enzyme's structure/denaturation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the active site loses its specific shape and can no longer bind the substrate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- enzyme activity decreases sharply after denaturation occurs <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for the role of allosteric regulation, which involves the binding of molecules to allosteric sites, a core component of non-competitive inhibition.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.1 Enzymes and metabolism > C1.1.16 Regulation of metabolic pathways by feedback inhibition (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.1.16 Regulation of metabolic pathways by feedback inhibition (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Allosteric regulation is the primary mechanism used in feedback inhibition to regulate metabolic pathways, which is a significant functional role of this type of regulation.

parts.content: Explain the role of allosteric regulation in enzyme activity.
parts.markscheme:
- Allosteric regulation involves a molecule binding to a site on the enzyme other than the active site (an allosteric site) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding at the allosteric site causes a conformational change in the enzyme <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The conformational change alters the shape/affinity of the active site for the substrate (therefore changing reaction rate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allosteric effectors can increase activity (activation) or decrease activity (inhibition) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example of an allosterically regulated enzyme (e.g. phosphofructokinase in glycolysis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct example detail (e.g. ATP inhibits PFK; AMP activates PFK) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how non-competitive inhibition affects the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Non-competitive inhibitor binds to an allosteric site, not the active site <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding changes enzyme conformation/active-site shape so catalysis is reduced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inhibition cannot be overcome by increasing substrate concentration (substrate still cannot restore full activity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maximum rate ($V_{max}$) is decreased because fewer functional enzyme molecules are available <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Substrate affinity/$K_m$ is unchanged in pure non-competitive inhibition (acceptable statement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how feedback inhibition can control the rate of a metabolic pathway, using a named example.
parts.markscheme:
- A metabolic pathway consists of a sequence of enzyme-catalysed reactions where the product of one step becomes the substrate for the next <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The end product acts as an inhibitor of an enzyme earlier in the pathway (often the first committed enzyme) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The end product binds allosterically to the regulatory enzyme, changing its conformation and decreasing activity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- When end-product concentration is high, enzyme activity decreases so pathway flux decreases and product formation slows <marks>1 mark</marks>
- When end-product concentration falls, inhibition is reduced and the pathway rate increases again (negative feedback/homeostasis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Named example given (e.g. ATP feedback inhibition of phosphofructokinase in glycolysis) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example correctly linked to regulation of pathway rate/ATP supply-demand balance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Any other valid feedback inhibition example clearly described (e.g. isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase) <marks>1 mark</marks>
